aboutPage.heading: About Max Mendes
bio: I'm Max, a frontend developer based in Częstochowa, Poland. I came into development through a year of focused self-study and have been building real products since 2025. I build modern business websites, e-commerce stores, and AI-powered web applications, with a focus on clean, maintainable code, strong performance, and real-world usability.

My role is technical: I write code and turn ideas into working products. I implement interfaces using proven UI patterns and component libraries like shadcn/ui and Tailwind CSS, with a strong focus on speed, SEO optimization, and scalability. I also integrate AI capabilities using OpenAI and Anthropic APIs, tools I've used in production to build features like smart content generation, automated workflows, and intelligent data processing. Design and branding are outside my scope. I focus entirely on development.

My process is structured and practical. I start with research: understanding the business goal, competitors, and technical requirements. Then I create low-fidelity prototypes in Figma to map user flows and features. After that, I build production-ready code using React and Next.js, handle deployment, optimization, and iteration based on feedback.

My biggest project, FlowMate, is an AI-powered email management platform I built solo for multi-platform broadcasting and smart content generation. It took 300+ hours and taught me how to architect a production SaaS end to end: authentication, payments, AI integration, and handling real user data. The product is now paused; the work is what shaped how I build.

I'm available for Polish businesses that need modern websites or online stores, as well as UK and US companies looking for a frontend developer. I'm fluent in English and work comfortably across time zones.

content.subtitle: Business websites coded from scratch in React and Next.js. No WordPress, no page builders, no plugin bills. A fast site that loads in under a second and is ready for Google from day one.

sections.title: How much does a custom website cost
paragraphs: Straight numbers, because most agencies make you book a call to hear them. A simple business website (5-7 pages, contact form, mobile-first) is $1,500-3,000 (approx. €1,400-2,800). A standard site with more pages, a second language or booking features runs $3,000-6,000 (€2,800-5,600). Larger builds with custom functionality sit at $6,000-12,000 (€5,600-11,000). You get an exact fixed quote after one conversation, and it does not grow mid-project.

Why the price is lower than a UK or US agency quote for the same work: I am one senior developer in Poland, not an office with project managers and account layers. You talk to the person writing the code. Same stack the agencies use, without their overhead.

links.text: Full breakdown: what a website really costs →

sections.title: What you get
paragraphs: A website designed and coded around your business: custom design matched to your brand, React and Next.js code, full responsiveness on phones and tablets, a contact form with email notifications, and analytics wired in so you can see where clients come from.

Also included, because it should never be an upsell: correct meta tags and structured data for Google, a sitemap, load time under a second, and deployment. The site is ready to rank from launch day, not "SEO-ready for a future package".

sections.title: Why not WordPress or a page builder
paragraphs: WordPress runs a huge share of business websites, and for some of them it is fine. The problems show up later: a dozen plugins to update, a premium theme with an annual fee, slow mobile loading, and break-ins through outdated plugins. You end up paying maintenance fees for a site that never changes.

A hand-coded site has no plugins, so there is nothing to update and nothing to break into. It loads faster because it ships no code it does not use. And there is no subscription: you pay once for the build, then only for the domain and hosting, typically $30-120 a year total. If your team publishes content daily and needs to edit it themselves, I wire a lightweight editor into exactly the sections that change, and nothing else.

links.text: Why HTML-first websites are winning again →

sections.title: Freelancer vs agency: what you are actually paying for
paragraphs: An agency quote pays for the account manager, the project manager, the office and the margin, and then for the developer who does the work. Hiring the developer directly removes every layer except the one that builds your site. That is the whole trick, and it is why the same site costs 40-60% less.

The honest trade-off: one person has one calendar. I take a limited number of projects and tell you real timelines upfront instead of overbooking. For most business websites, one senior developer end to end beats a junior team with a manager in the middle.

sections.title: How I work and how long it takes
paragraphs: The process is short. First a conversation about what the site needs to do for the business: calls, bookings, quote requests. You get a fixed quote and timeline the next working day. Then design for approval, code, your revisions, launch.

A simple business site takes 1-2 weeks from design approval. Bigger or multilingual builds take 3-4 weeks. The most common delay in any web project is waiting on content and photos, so if you do not have them, I help produce them.

faq.question: How much does a custom website cost?
faq.answer: From me: $1,500-3,000 for a simple business site, $3,000-6,000 standard, $6,000-12,000 for complex builds (roughly €1,400-11,000). US and UK agencies quote 2-3x that for the same scope. The price depends on page count, features and whether your content is ready. See the [full cost breakdown](/en/blog/website-cost-small-business-poland).

faq.question: How much does a website cost for a small business?
faq.answer: Most small businesses need the simple tier: 5-7 pages, contact form, local SEO basics. That is $1,500-3,000 with me, coded from scratch. Ongoing costs stay near $30-120 a year (domain plus hosting) because there are no plugin licenses or maintenance retainers to keep paying.

faq.question: Is it cheaper to hire a freelancer or an agency for a website?
faq.answer: A freelancer, usually by half or more, because you are not funding project managers and office overhead. The risk people worry about is reliability, which is why I work with fixed quotes, real timelines and a public portfolio of live sites you can click through before deciding.

faq.question: How long does it take to build a website?
faq.answer: A simple business website: 1-2 weeks from approved design. Standard builds: 3-4 weeks. What actually stretches timelines is content: text, photos, offers. I flag everything I need in week one, and help write it if it does not exist yet.

faq.question: Should I choose WordPress or a custom-coded website?
faq.answer: For most service businesses, custom-coded wins: faster, safer, and no plugin or theme fees. WordPress makes sense when a team publishes lots of content daily and must self-edit everything. I wrote up the comparison honestly in [the HTML-first article](/en/blog/html-first-websites-winning-again), including when I would not recommend custom.

faq.question: How much do freelance web developers charge in Europe?
faq.answer: Public rate surveys (Arc, Index.dev) put European freelance rates at roughly €15-90 an hour depending on country and seniority, with Polish seniors typically well below UK and German rates for equivalent skill. I quote fixed project prices instead of hourly, so you know the total before work starts and overrun risk sits with me, not you.

faq.question: What is included in the cost of a custom website?
faq.answer: Design, code, responsive layouts, contact forms, analytics, technical SEO (meta tags, structured data, sitemap), deployment and a handover walkthrough. Not included: domain and hosting (you own both, typically $30-120 a year) and content writing, which I can add if needed. No hidden retainers.

faq.question: Is a custom-coded website faster than WordPress?
faq.answer: Almost always, and by a lot. A hand-coded site ships only the code it uses, while a typical WordPress build drags a theme and a dozen plugins into every page load. Speed shows up in Google rankings via [Core Web Vitals](/en/services/seo-performance-optimization) and in how many visitors stay.

faq.question: How much does web development cost in Poland?
faq.answer: Poland-based development runs far below UK, German or US pricing for the same seniority, which is exactly why UK and EU companies hire here. You get the rate advantage without outsourcing roulette: I am one accountable developer with live projects you can inspect, working in your timezone.

sections.title: Website or web app: which one are you actually building
paragraphs: A website shows information: your offer, your contact details, your pricing. A web application does work: users log in, enter data, the system processes it, bills it, reports on it and enforces permissions. If your idea involves user accounts, a dashboard, recurring payments or business logic, you are building an application.
The distinction matters because it changes the architecture, the timeline and the budget. If what you need is a marketing site, see custom website development. This page is about software.

sections.title: How much does it cost to build a SaaS MVP
paragraphs: Real ranges, not "it depends". An MVP with the core feature, authentication and a simple dashboard starts at $8,000-10,000 (approx. €7,400-9,300) with me. A standard MVP with payments, roles and an admin panel runs $10,000-25,000. Complex systems with heavy integrations go beyond that, and I say so before we start, not after. Agency-built MVPs are commonly quoted in six figures, which is exactly the overhead gap a senior solo developer removes.

What drives cost is specific: authentication and roles, payments (especially subscriptions), third-party integrations, and reporting dashboards. The winning strategy is almost always a tight MVP first, then building based on how real users behave instead of guesses.

sections.title: What I build
paragraphs: Complete systems: registration and login (including Google sign-in), roles and permissions, live dashboards, one-off and recurring payments through Stripe, email and push notifications, and API integrations with the tools you already run.

Everything is TypeScript with tests, on a boring-on-purpose stack: React and Next.js on the front, Node.js and PostgreSQL behind. These are the technologies the biggest web products run on, and they are standard enough that any future developer can take the project over. No lock-in to me.

sections.title: Proof: products I have shipped
paragraphs: Fennaro: a dog-matching app I built solo, end to end. Accounts, geographic matching on PostGIS, chat, moderation, GDPR compliance and an installable PWA, live in production. FlowMate: a SaaS email platform where I learned production architecture the hard way: secure auth, async processing, AI integrations. BestEats: a food-ranking platform live in Portugal, where I ship code under senior code review.
I write about these builds publicly, including the mistakes. If you want to know how I think before hiring me, read the Fennaro story: it covers the decisions no portfolio screenshot shows.

links.text: How I built Fennaro: the full story →

sections.title: How delivery works and how long it takes
paragraphs: We start from the problem, not the feature list: what the system must accomplish, who uses it, what is genuinely essential in version one. You get an MVP scope with a fixed quote, split into stages so you always know what you are paying for. A typical MVP ships in 4-8 weeks with weekly progress updates you can react to.

After launch you are not stranded: the code is documented, deployed on infrastructure you own, and monitored, so I know about errors before your users report them. We grow the product in stages based on real usage.

content.faqTitle: SaaS and web app questions, answered

faq.question: How much does it cost to build a SaaS MVP?
faq.answer: With me: from $8,000-10,000 for a core-feature MVP with auth and a dashboard, $10,000-25,000 with payments and roles (roughly €7,400-23,000). Agency cost surveys put the median agency-built MVP around $120,000, which is the overhead you are not paying when you hire the developer directly.

faq.question: How much does it cost to build a SaaS app?
faq.answer: A full production SaaS beyond MVP stage typically lands at $25,000+ depending on integrations, compliance and scale needs. I quote in stages, so you fund the MVP first, verify real demand, and only then invest in the full build. Nobody should pay full-product money on a guess.

faq.question: How long does it take to build a SaaS MVP?
faq.answer: A focused MVP ships in 4-8 weeks with me. Typical agency timelines run 8-16 weeks, and the difference is layers: no handoffs between designer, PM and developer. The biggest real-world delay is unclear requirements, which is why week one is scope work, not code.

faq.question: Should I hire a freelancer or an agency to build my SaaS MVP?
faq.answer: For MVPs and small-to-mid systems, a senior freelancer is usually the better deal: direct communication and no agency overhead. For very large systems a team makes sense, and I say that in the quote instead of overselling. Judge any developer by shipped products, not slide decks. Mine are [public](/en/projects).

faq.question: What features does a SaaS MVP actually need?
faq.answer: The one core feature that proves your idea, plus login, a minimal dashboard and a way to pay you. That is usually it. Every extra feature before real users arrive is money spent on a guess. The point of an MVP is to make the next spending decision with evidence.

faq.question: How much do freelance SaaS developers charge?
faq.answer: Marketplace listings (Upwork, Arc) show senior SaaS developers at $50-120 an hour, with Poland-based seniors at the lower half of that for equal skill. I price fixed per stage instead of hourly: you know the number before work starts, and scope creep is my risk to manage, not your invoice surprise.

faq.question: What is a realistic SaaS timeline for a non-technical founder?
faq.answer: Plan for 4-8 weeks of build after about a week of scope definition, then 2-4 weeks of real-user feedback before deciding the next stage. The classic founder mistake is skipping the feedback window and ordering more features immediately. The system I hand over is built to be extended, so waiting costs nothing.

faq.question: What is the difference between an MVP and a full SaaS product?
faq.answer: An MVP proves that people want the core of your idea; a full product earns money at scale. The MVP has one polished core flow; the full product adds roles, billing edge cases, admin tooling and integrations. Funding them as separate stages protects you from building the second one for users who never arrive.

faq.question: How much does web app development cost in Poland?
faq.answer: Complex custom web applications from Polish teams are quoted at roughly $25,000-100,000+ in 2026, still well under Western European agency pricing. As a solo senior developer I sit below team pricing for MVP-scale work: $8,000-25,000 covers most first versions, with the same stack and standards.

content.subtitle: Custom online stores built with React for speed and conversions. Designed around your catalog and business logic, with full control over every detail.

sections.title: When a template store stops being enough
paragraphs: Custom e-commerce development means your online store is built specifically for how your business operates. Standard platforms work well at the start, but many businesses outgrow them when pricing rules become complex, inventory logic is non-standard, or integrations are required.

A custom online store allows full control over performance, SEO, and business logic. With React and Next.js, product pages load faster and meet Core Web Vitals standards, factors that directly affect conversion rates and search visibility.

sections.title: Everything the store ships with
paragraphs: I build e-commerce platforms that cover the entire sales flow. Projects can include a structured product catalog with inventory management, a streamlined shopping cart, and a checkout process tailored to your users.

Payment processing is integrated flexibly, supporting both international methods (Stripe) and Polish payment processors (Przelewy24, PayU). Order management features handle fulfillment status and customer communication. Customer accounts provide order history and saved details.

Advanced search and filtering help users find products quickly, while a custom admin dashboard allows you to manage products, orders, and inventory efficiently. Automated email notifications handle order confirmations and shipping updates without manual work.

sections.title: Is this you: signs you have outgrown your platform
paragraphs: You know this moment if you are in it: the pricing rules your sales team actually uses cannot be expressed in the platform admin. The ERP sync works "mostly". Checkout needs one step the template will not allow. Every workaround is another app subscription, and the store gets slower with each one.

That is the customer this service exists for: B2B wholesale with custom pricing and volume discounts, stores that need real integrations with accounting or inventory systems, configurable products with many variants, and international selling with multi-currency. If none of that describes you yet, a platform is still the right answer, and I will say so.

sections.title: Why speed is a sales feature, not a tech score
paragraphs: Every extra second of load time measurably costs orders, and mobile shoppers are the least patient. A custom React storefront ships only the code it needs, so product pages stay fast even with a large catalog. Server-side rendering means Google indexes every product properly, and structured data makes listings eligible for price and rating snippets in search results.

This is the same production discipline I use on consumer platforms like BestEats, live in Portugal, where page speed and catalog UX decide whether people stay. The stack and standards carry over one-to-one to commerce.

sections.title: How much does a custom online store cost
paragraphs: A custom store built from scratch starts around $8,000 (approx. €7,400) with me and scales with catalog complexity and integrations. Compare the running costs: platform stores pay subscription plus apps plus transaction fees forever, while a custom store pays only payment processing. For stores doing real volume, the custom build usually pays for itself within 2-3 years, and you own it outright.

The common trigger points for going custom: platform fees start to hurt, the checkout needs to work differently than the template allows, or you need integrations (inventory, ERP, custom pricing rules) the app store cannot deliver cleanly. If a platform still fits your stage, I will say so instead of selling you a build you do not need yet.

faq.question: How much does a custom e-commerce website cost?
faq.answer: From me: starting around $8,000, scaling with catalog size and integrations (agencies quote $25,000-150,000+ for comparable builds). Unlike platform stores there is no monthly platform fee, no app subscriptions and no platform transaction cut: you pay payment processing only, and you own the store.

faq.question: Shopify or custom e-commerce: which is better?
faq.answer: Shopify is a fine start: fast to launch, predictable monthly cost. Custom wins when fees bite at scale, when the checkout must differ from the template, or when integrations get hairy. The 2-3 year total cost usually favors custom for stores with real volume. I will tell you honestly which side you are on.

faq.question: What is a good Shopify alternative for a custom store?
faq.answer: For merchants who have outgrown templates, the strongest alternative is a custom React storefront with a headless backend: full control of the buying flow, no platform fees, top speed scores. It is an investment rather than a subscription, which is exactly why it suits stores past the experiment stage.

faq.question: How much does headless e-commerce cost?
faq.answer: Published 2026 ranges run $20,000-150,000+ from agencies. A solo senior build starts well under that, from around $8,000 for a focused store, because you are not paying a team-sized margin. The price driver is integrations: inventory systems, ERP, custom pricing logic, multi-currency.

faq.question: Does page speed affect e-commerce conversion rates?
faq.answer: In e-commerce it is the difference between a sale and an abandoned cart: slow product pages bleed mobile buyers before they ever reach checkout. A custom React storefront stays fast because it carries no app bloat. For fixing speed on an existing store, see [technical SEO and performance](/en/services/seo-performance-optimization).

faq.question: How much does it cost to hire an e-commerce developer in Poland?
faq.answer: Poland-based e-commerce builds are quoted at $10,000-50,000+ for full custom stores, well below UK and US agency pricing for equivalent work. You get senior-level engineering at Central European rates, in a European timezone, with direct communication instead of an account-manager relay.

faq.question: What is the best e-commerce platform for a small business?
faq.answer: If you are validating a product idea, start on a platform: cheap, fast, disposable. Move to custom when the numbers prove the business: real order volume, painful fees, integration needs. The worst outcome is paying custom-build money before you know anyone wants the products.

faq.question: Why do businesses outgrow Shopify or WooCommerce?
faq.answer: Three patterns repeat: pricing or checkout logic the template cannot express, apps and plugins stacking up in cost and slowing the store down, and integrations with inventory or ERP systems that never quite work. When two of the three appear, it is time to price a [custom build](/en/contact).

seo.ogTitle: SEO & Performance Optimization - Faster Sites | Max Mendes

schema.description: Performance optimization and SEO focused on real business results. Faster page speed improves rankings, conversions, and revenue through Core Web Vitals optimization.

content.subtitle: Your site is slow, or invisible in Google, and you want it fixed in the code instead of managed in a monthly report. That is exactly what this service does.

sections.title: Why performance and SEO matter for business
paragraphs: Website performance and SEO are directly tied to business outcomes. Slow websites lose users before they convert, while search engines rank faster, more reliable pages higher. Google's Core Web Vitals are official ranking factors, which means page speed and stability affect how visible your site is in search results.

Even small delays matter. Independent studies from NitroPack and HubSpot put the cost at roughly 4% of conversions per extra second of load time, and mobile users are hit hardest. With mobile-first indexing, Google primarily evaluates the mobile version of your site, making mobile performance critical. The goal of optimization is not technical perfection for its own sake, but more qualified traffic, better user engagement, and higher conversion rates.

sections.title: The speed work: what actually gets faster
paragraphs: I focus on measurable improvements to how fast and stable your website feels to real users. This includes improving Core Web Vitals metrics such as LCP (Largest Contentful Paint), FID/INP (interaction responsiveness), and CLS (layout stability).

Page speed is optimized through image compression, modern formats, lazy loading, and reducing unnecessary JavaScript. Code splitting and asset optimization ensure users load only what they need. Caching strategies and CDN usage reduce server response times, while database queries are reviewed to eliminate bottlenecks. Each project includes before-and-after measurements using tools like Lighthouse and real-user metrics, so improvements are clear and verifiable.

sections.title: The Google work: crawling, indexing, structured data
paragraphs: SEO optimization starts with a technical audit to identify crawlability and indexing issues that limit visibility. I address problems such as broken internal links, duplicate content signals, incorrect redirects, and poor URL structures.

Structured data (Schema.org) is implemented to improve eligibility for rich results and enhanced listings. Meta titles and descriptions are optimized for clarity, relevance, and click-through rate, not keyword stuffing. Server-side rendering and correct handling of JavaScript ensure search engines can fully index your content. The result is a technically sound foundation that supports long-term SEO growth.

sections.title: Is this your website?
paragraphs: The pattern I see most: the site looks fine, the content is decent, and it still gets no Google traffic. Sometimes it loads in five seconds on a phone. Sometimes half the pages were never indexed and nobody noticed. Sometimes a redesign quietly broke redirects a year ago and rankings never recovered. If any of that sounds familiar, this is the service that finds out which one is happening to you, with evidence.

You are looking at my proof right now: this portfolio runs the same stack and the same techniques, loads in well under a second on a normal connection, and every fix I sell is one I use on my own pages first.

links.text: How I build for speed: the HTML-first argument →

sections.title: Audit first, fixes second, numbers to prove it
paragraphs: The process is deliberately boring: a full technical audit of performance and indexing, a prioritized fix list, implementation, and before-and-after validation in the same tools Google uses (Search Console, PageSpeed). No agency layers, no reselling: the person who audits your site is the person who fixes the code.

Fair warning on expectations: rankings move over weeks, not days, and anyone promising page one in seven days is not telling the truth. What I can show quickly is the technical delta: speed scores, indexing status, structured data validity.

sections.title: What a technical SEO audit costs
paragraphs: A technical audit with a prioritized fix list starts at $600 (approx. €550) depending on site size. You get a document a business owner can read: what is broken, what it costs you, what to fix first. The audit stands alone, so you can implement it with any developer, not just me.

Implementation is quoted after the audit, because the audit is what reveals the scope. A typical audit-plus-key-fixes package lands between $1,500 and $4,000. One-time work, not a retainer: I do not sell monthly SEO subscriptions, link building or sponsored posts, and if your site mainly needs content rather than code, the audit says so plainly.

cta.subtitle: Tell me what worries you: no enquiries, a traffic drop, slow loading. You get an initial technical read and an audit quote by the next working day, no commitment.

faq.question: How much does a technical SEO audit cost?
faq.answer: From me: from $600, scaling with site size, delivered as a prioritized fix list you can implement with anyone. Market pricing runs from a few hundred dollars for automated scans to $30,000 for enterprise audits. Mine is manual, code-level, and readable by a business owner, not just a developer.

faq.question: How much does an SEO audit cost from a freelancer?
faq.answer: Published freelancer pricing runs $300-2,500 per audit or $50-150 hourly. I price fixed from $600 so the number is known upfront. The thing to check is what you get: a screenshot of an automated tool is not an audit. You should receive specific, prioritized fixes with the reasoning behind each.

faq.question: Why is my website not ranking on Google?
faq.answer: The usual technical suspects: slow loading (especially mobile), pages Google cannot properly read, duplicate content competing with itself, and indexing errors. Quick self-check: search "site:yourdomain.com". If key pages are missing, Google has not indexed them, and that is a code problem no content plan will fix.

faq.question: How much does Core Web Vitals optimization cost?
faq.answer: Agency service pages quote $5,000-50,000+. Realistic solo pricing for most business sites: the $1,500-4,000 audit-plus-fixes range, because the wins are usually concentrated in images, fonts, JavaScript weight and loading order. You see before-and-after numbers from the same tools Google uses.

faq.question: How often should I get an SEO audit?
faq.answer: Most practitioners advise every 3-6 months for active sites, and always after a redesign, migration or platform change, which is where technical SEO usually breaks. If nothing changed on the site and traffic is stable, an annual check is honest advice, and cheaper than a subscription.

faq.question: Does page speed really affect conversion rate?
faq.answer: Yes, measurably: NitroPack and HubSpot studies show conversions dropping roughly 4% per extra second of load time, with mobile hit hardest. Speed also feeds Core Web Vitals, which Google uses in ranking. Faster site, better positions, and more of the visitors you already earn actually converting.

faq.question: What is included in a technical SEO audit?
faq.answer: Indexing status per page (and why anything is excluded), crawl and canonical issues, Core Web Vitals with the specific causes, structured data validation, hreflang for multilingual sites, and redirect health. Each finding comes prioritized by impact, so you fix what moves rankings first instead of everything at once.

faq.question: Can a slow website hurt my Google rankings?
faq.answer: Yes. Core Web Vitals are an official ranking signal, and speed affects rankings indirectly too: slow pages get abandoned, and Google reads that behavior. The bigger cost is usually commercial, since visitors leave before the page finishes loading. Fixing speed pays twice: rankings and conversions.

seo.ogTitle: AI Integration - Intelligent Features | Max Mendes

schema.description: AI-powered features for web applications including chatbots, content generation, and workflow automation using OpenAI and Anthropic APIs.

content.subtitle: Practical AI for products and processes you already run. If a feature cannot pay for itself, I tell you before you spend anything.

sections.title: What AI can actually do for you (and what it cannot)
paragraphs: AI integration means adding focused, intelligent features to products you already run, not rebuilding everything or chasing trends. In practice, AI can help automate repetitive work, classify and route information, improve search relevance, generate summaries, or assist users with clearer responses. The difference between useful AI and "AI for AI's sake" is intent: each feature starts with a specific problem. The goal is to reduce manual effort, improve user experience, or unlock a capability that wasn't feasible before. This is not about replacing teams or promising perfect accuracy. It is about practical gains where machine learning genuinely adds value.

sections.title: The features I build most often
paragraphs: Projects can include text classification and categorization, content generation and summarization, and intelligent search with semantic matching. Other frequent needs are recommendations and personalization, image recognition and processing, and natural language processing for user input. Automated responses and chatbots are used when appropriate, typically as assistance, not full automation. Data analysis and pattern recognition can surface trends or anomalies that are hard to detect with rules alone. Each feature is scoped to a clear outcome, measured against real usage, and designed to fail safely.

sections.title: Technical implementation and providers
paragraphs: I integrate with established AI providers such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google, as well as selected open-source models when appropriate. Work includes API integration, prompt design for consistent outputs, and cost management so usage stays predictable. Error handling and fallbacks are essential. AI is probabilistic and needs guardrails. Data privacy, security, and performance are considered from the start, including caching and asynchronous processing.

In FlowMate, the SaaS platform I built end to end, I implemented AI integrations for email classification and smart response suggestions that had to handle the real-world messiness of actual inboxes. I also ship AI features in MarkMind, a bookmark manager with 700+ active users.

links.text: See the AI work on the Projects page →

sections.title: Is this you: where AI earns its keep
paragraphs: The businesses that get real returns from AI share a symptom: somebody spends hours doing formulaic text work. Answering the same customer questions. Copying data out of documents into a system. Sorting and routing enquiries. Tagging and summarizing content. If a task is repetitive and lives in text, it is a candidate, and we can usually measure the saved hours within weeks of a pilot.

Support teams that want AI drafting answers without replacing humans, product catalogs that need real search, and content operations drowning in categorization are the typical wins. Implemented narrowly, with clear limits, and measured.

links.text: Questions about AI integration costs or process? Check the FAQ →

sections.title: Start narrow, measure, then scale
paragraphs: I start with a narrow use case, not "add AI everywhere." A small proof of concept validates whether AI actually improves the workflow. Implementation is gradual, with fallbacks when results are uncertain. Costs are tracked early. AI API calls scale with usage. If a rule-based solution is better, I recommend it. The key question is always the same: will this intelligent feature materially improve the product?

sections.title: What AI integration costs
paragraphs: A focused integration, such as a chatbot answering from your actual documents or automating one process, starts at $3,000 (approx. €2,800). Deeper integrations wired into your existing systems, with an admin panel and answer-quality controls, typically run $8,000-20,000. You get a staged quote after one conversation about the process you want to improve.

On top of the build there is API usage, paid directly to the provider (OpenAI or Anthropic): for typical small-business volume that is $20-200 a month. I show these numbers before we start and set spending limits in the code, so the bill cannot surprise you.

faq.question: How much does it cost to add an AI chatbot to a website?
faq.answer: Subscription chatbot tools run $5-500 a month and answer generically. A custom chatbot that answers from your documents and admits what it does not know starts at $3,000 with me, plus API usage (typically $20-200 monthly at small-business volume, with hard spending limits built in).

faq.question: Is AI integration worth it for a small business?
faq.answer: Worth it where the numbers work: repetitive customer questions, manual data entry, documents waiting to be processed. Not worth it for the sake of being modern. That is why the first conversation is free: sometimes the honest answer is that AI does not fit your case yet, and you will hear it.

faq.question: How much does OpenAI API integration cost for a business?
faq.answer: Two costs: the build (from $3,000 for a focused integration) and usage paid to OpenAI. Real example scale: a support bot answering about 10,000 queries a month costs roughly $150 monthly in API fees on a current model. I set usage caps in code so costs stay predictable.

faq.question: Should I use ChatGPT or Claude for my business?
faq.answer: It depends on the job: both have commercial APIs suitable for business integrations, with comparable data protections. I build provider-agnostic, pick the model per task on quality and cost, and can switch later without rebuilding, so you are choosing an architecture, not marrying a vendor.

faq.question: Custom AI chatbot or an off-the-shelf tool: which is better?
faq.answer: Off-the-shelf is fine for generic FAQs and costs a subscription. Custom wins when the bot must know your actual products, policies and documents, integrate with your systems, and refuse to invent answers. The build costs more upfront and stops paying rent forever, which flips the math over time.

faq.question: What can AI actually automate for a small business?
faq.answer: The repetitive text work: answering the same customer questions, categorizing and summarizing email, pulling data out of documents and invoices, writing product descriptions, searching internal knowledge. The tell: if an employee does something formulaic for hours, it is a good automation candidate.

faq.question: Is it safe to give an AI chatbot access to my business data?
faq.answer: Done properly, yes. Commercial APIs from OpenAI and Anthropic state in their business terms that API data is not used for training. I add data minimization (the model sees only what it needs), logging of what was sent, and GDPR-conscious architecture. The consumer ChatGPT app is a different, riskier thing.

faq.question: How much does a custom AI chatbot cost compared to a subscription tool?
faq.answer: A subscription tool: $5-500 monthly, forever, answering generically. A custom build: from $3,000 once, plus modest API usage, answering from your actual business knowledge. If the bot matters to revenue or support load, custom usually wins within the first year, and you own it.

schema.description: Frequently asked questions about web development services, pricing, timelines, and technical details.

faqPage.intro: How we work together, what to expect, and what happens after launch.

items.question: What services do you provide?
items.answer: I build production web applications: business websites, SaaS platforms, custom e-commerce systems, and AI-powered features. Everything is built with React, Next.js, and TypeScript. Production-ready from day one, built to scale.

items.question: What don't you build?
items.answer: I focus exclusively on custom software development: websites, web apps, and SaaS built with React and Next.js. Design, branding, and marketing are outside my scope. If you need those services, I can recommend specialists.

items.question: Do you work solo or with a team?
items.answer: I work as a solo full-stack developer and handle the entire build myself. You work directly with the person writing your code, one point of contact from start to finish.

items.question: Can you handle both Polish and international clients?
items.answer: Yes. I work with companies in Poland and international clients in the UK and US. Communication is in English or Polish, and Polish content is reviewed with my wife to ensure natural language and accuracy.

items.question: How do I know a web developer is legitimate before I pay a deposit?
items.answer: Ask for addresses of live sites, not screenshots, and open them on your phone: speed and polish are hard to fake. Check whether the developer writes publicly about their work over time, because a long paper trail is the opposite of a scam profile. Confirm in writing who will own the domain and code. And notice whether they ask questions about your business or just send a price. I built my public portfolio and blog precisely so clients can run these checks before we ever talk.

items.question: Can one developer really build an entire web app on their own?
items.answer: Yes, and I can point at proof rather than promise: Fennaro, a dog-matching app with accounts, geolocation matching, chat, moderation and GDPR compliance, built by me end to end and live in production. Modern tooling makes a senior solo developer genuinely capable of shipping complete products. The honest limit is scale: very large systems need teams, and I say so in the quote when a project crosses that line.

items.question: What is the difference between a web developer and a web designer?
items.answer: A designer decides how the site looks and feels; a developer makes it work: fast loading, working forms, clean code Google can read. A lot of disappointing websites had only one of the two. I cover both sides for typical business projects, which means one person accountable for the final result instead of a designer and a developer pointing at each other when something breaks.

items.question: Do you sign an NDA before discussing my project?
items.answer: Yes, if you need one signed before sharing details, that is standard practice for product ideas and I have no problem with it. Honest context: for a typical business website an NDA is rarely necessary since nothing secret changes hands. Either way, your project details never appear in my portfolio, posts or case studies without your explicit agreement.

items.question: What happens if you get sick or become unavailable during my project?
items.answer: A fair question to ask any solo developer. My answers: your code lives in a repository you can access from week one, so work never disappears with me. I build in standard technologies (React, Next.js, TypeScript) that any developer on the market can pick up without reverse-engineering. And if a longer absence ever happens, the timeline moves openly instead of the project going quiet.

items.question: How do we start working together?
items.answer: We start with a conversation about your problem and goals. I review the requirements, ask technical questions, and propose a clear scope, timeline, and pricing before any work begins.
items.linkText: Get in touch to discuss your project

items.question: What's your development process?
items.answer: I start by defining requirements and technical architecture, then move into iterative development with regular checkpoints. Features are built and tested as we go, with adjustments based on your feedback.

items.question: How long does a typical project take?
items.answer: Simple business websites take 3-5 weeks. SaaS platforms or custom e-commerce systems typically take 8-12 weeks, depending on integrations, database complexity, and feature requirements.

items.question: How do we communicate during the project?
items.answer: Direct communication via email, Slack, or calls, whatever works for you. Weekly check-ins keep you updated on progress, blockers, and next steps. No unnecessary meetings or status reports.

items.question: How long does it take to get a quote or proposal?
items.answer: One working day for most projects, occasionally two for complex systems. You describe the project, I come back with a fixed price and a real timeline, not a vague range designed to start a negotiation. The quote is free and carries no obligation. If anything is unclear, I ask before quoting instead of padding the number to cover my own uncertainty.

items.question: What information do you need from me before we start?
items.answer: Less than you might fear: what your business does, who the site or app is for, what should happen when someone lands on it, and any examples you like or hate. That usually fits in one conversation. Content (text, photos, offers) is the piece clients underestimate, so I flag everything needed in week one and help produce whatever does not exist yet.

items.question: How many rounds of revisions are included?
items.answer: The contract states it plainly: typically two to three revision rounds at the design stage and one to two after implementation. Just as important is the definition: a revision adjusts what we agreed, while a new page or feature is new scope, priced before I build it. That distinction, in writing, is what keeps projects friendly. Unlimited-revisions promises usually hide either padding in the price or a fight at the end.

items.question: What if I want to change the scope after we have started?
items.answer: It happens in most projects and it is fine. Small adjustments fold into the agreed revision rounds. Bigger additions get their own quote before any work starts, so you decide with the price in front of you. What never happens: silent extras appearing on the final invoice. Scope changes are a conversation, not a surprise.

items.question: Do you work with clients in different time zones?
items.answer: Constantly: I am in Poland (CET), which overlaps the full UK and EU working day and the US East Coast morning. In practice you get answers within hours, not days, and most collaboration is asynchronous anyway: short written updates you can read whenever suits you, with calls only when they genuinely move things forward.

items.question: What does the final handover look like?
items.answer: We walk through the finished site together: every page, form, mobile view and speed check. You receive all access credentials (domain, hosting, analytics), the code repository, written confirmation of the rights transfer, and a short walkthrough of how to manage the basics. Only after your sign-off does the project go live. Early wobbles in the first weeks get fixed under warranty without a debate about whose fault they are.

items.question: What's your pricing structure?
items.answer: Most projects are fixed-price based on a clearly defined scope. For ongoing development or evolving products, I offer monthly development retainers.

items.question: Do you require a deposit?
items.answer: Yes. Projects start with a 30-50% deposit, with the remainder paid in milestones or at delivery. This keeps timelines predictable and protects both sides.

items.question: What payment methods do you accept?
items.answer: I accept bank transfers (SWIFT/SEPA for international clients) and online payment platforms. All transactions are straightforward and compliant with EU regulations.

items.question: Can I pay in installments?
items.answer: Yes, for larger projects. Payments are split into clear milestones tied to delivered work, so you're paying for real progress.

items.question: Is it cheaper to pay hourly or fixed price for a website project?
items.answer: Fixed price protects you better, and it is how I work. On hourly billing the overrun risk is yours: every delay costs you money. On a fixed quote the risk is mine, which gives me every incentive to work efficiently and scope carefully upfront. Hourly makes sense only for small unpredictable fixes on existing projects, and when that is the honest answer, I say so.

items.question: What happens to my deposit if I need to cancel the project?
items.answer: If you cancel before I have started, you get it back: I do not keep money for work that never happened. If work is underway, we settle for the completed stage and you keep everything produced so far, including the code. The exact terms sit in the contract before you sign, so there is never an argument about it later.

items.question: Do I need a contract for a website project?
items.answer: Yes, always, even for small projects, and be wary of anyone who prefers not to have one. The contract is what makes the deliverables, price, timeline and ownership enforceable instead of implied. Mine is short and readable: scope, fixed price, schedule, revision rounds, rights transfer, access handover. Ten minutes of reading that prevents the classic horror stories.

items.question: What should be included in a web development contract?
items.answer: Minimum: exact scope (pages, features, languages), fixed price with a payment schedule, a realistic deadline, the number of revision rounds, transfer of code and design rights to you on payment, and the list of credentials you receive. A useful test for any developer: ask for these clauses and watch the reaction. Mine are standard, and that is verifiable before you commit.

items.question: What tech stack do you use?
items.answer: React, Next.js, and TypeScript for frontend and full-stack applications. PostgreSQL or Supabase for databases, with additional tools based on your specific requirements. This stack delivers fast, secure, maintainable software that scales without major rewrites.

items.question: Can you work with my existing codebase?
items.answer: Yes, if the codebase is reasonably maintained and documented. I'll review it first and tell you honestly whether it makes sense to extend it, refactor parts, or start fresh.

items.question: Do you handle hosting and deployment?
items.answer: Yes. I handle production deployment, environment configuration, and performance optimization. Your application launches stable, monitored, and ready for real users, not just pushed live and forgotten.

items.question: How do you ensure my site is secure and performant?
items.answer: I follow modern security practices for authentication and data handling. Server-side rendering, optimized images, and proper caching are built in by default. Core Web Vitals and load times are treated as requirements, not extras.

items.question: Can you take over and continue a project built by another developer?
items.answer: Yes, regularly, but always starting with a code review, because inherited projects range from "just finish it" to "cheaper to rebuild than rescue". After the review you get a straight assessment with both paths priced. One requirement: access to the code and hosting. If your previous developer will not hand it over, that is an ownership problem to solve first, and I will help you name it plainly.

items.question: Is my website GDPR compliant? What do I actually need?
items.answer: For a typical business site: a privacy policy, consent on forms, correctly configured analytics, and a cookie banner where one is genuinely required (not everywhere). These are legal obligations for anyone serving EU or UK visitors, and I build them into the code rather than bolting on plugins. Caveat: I am a developer, not a lawyer, so unusual data processing deserves a legal review on top.

items.question: What does "production-ready" actually mean?
items.answer: It means the thing survives contact with reality: errors are handled instead of crashing, data is validated, backups and monitoring exist, security headers are set, and the site keeps working when traffic arrives or an API hiccups. A demo shows a feature working once on the developer's machine. Production-ready means it works for strangers, at scale, unattended. That gap is where a lot of cheap builds quietly fail.

items.question: Will my website work well on mobile devices?
items.answer: It is built for mobile first, then scaled up, because over half of real traffic is phones and Google evaluates the mobile version of your site as the primary one. Every project I ship is tested across real device sizes for readability, tap targets and speed on slower connections. If your current site needs pinch-zooming to read, you are losing customers daily, measurably.

items.question: Does my website need an SSL certificate?
items.answer: Yes, without exception: browsers mark sites without SSL as "not secure", visitors bounce, and Google demotes them. The padlock encrypts everything between the visitor and your site. On modern hosting SSL is free and renews automatically, and it is configured from day one on everything I build. If someone quotes SSL as a paid extra, treat it as a red flag about the rest of the offer.

items.question: How much do AI features cost in practice?
items.answer: AI integration has two parts: one-time development cost and ongoing API usage costs. Development covers designing, integrating, and testing the AI feature, while API costs depend on how often users trigger AI actions. I explain both upfront so there are no surprises later.

items.question: How are AI costs kept predictable and under control?
items.answer: AI costs are managed through caching, request limits, and only calling AI when it adds real value. Common results can be reused instead of regenerated, which significantly reduces usage. I also design features so costs scale with business value, not background noise.

items.question: What happens to my data when using AI providers?
items.answer: Data sent to AI providers is processed by external services like OpenAI or Anthropic, so they technically see the input. I minimize what is sent, avoid unnecessary personal data, and follow provider privacy options where available. If strict data control is required, we discuss alternatives or limits before building anything.

items.question: How reliable and accurate is AI in real products?
items.answer: AI is probabilistic, not deterministic, so it will sometimes be wrong or inconsistent. That's why I always build fallbacks, validation, and clear UI states for uncertain results. You should expect AI to assist and speed things up, not to be 100% correct.

items.question: Which AI provider should I use: OpenAI, Anthropic, or something else?
items.answer: The best provider depends on the task, cost profile, and reliability requirements. Some models are better at structured classification, others at longer reasoning or safer outputs. I usually test providers on your specific use case and choose based on results, not brand names.

items.question: How do I know if my business is actually ready for AI?
items.answer: Look for one signal: repetitive text work that eats hours. Answering the same customer questions, retyping data from documents, sorting enquiries. If that exists, a small pilot can prove value in weeks. If it does not, you are probably not ready, and no tool will change that. Readiness is about having a measurable process to improve, not about company size or budget.

items.question: Do I need to tell my customers they are talking to an AI chatbot?
items.answer: Increasingly yes: disclosure requirements are appearing across jurisdictions (several US states already have them, and the EU AI Act pushes the same way), and beyond the law it is simply good practice, because users who discover a hidden bot lose trust in the brand. The bots I build identify themselves and hand off to a human cleanly. Transparency costs nothing and protects you legally and reputationally.

items.question: Who is liable if my AI chatbot gives a customer wrong information?
items.answer: You are: courts have already held companies responsible for their chatbots' promises (the Air Canada case is the famous precedent, where a tribunal ordered the airline to honor its bot's invented refund policy). That is exactly why I build bots that answer only from your approved documents, admit when they do not know, and log every conversation, so the risk is engineered down instead of ignored.

items.question: What happens after the project launches?
items.answer: I monitor stability after launch, fix any early issues, and ensure everything works as expected. You're not left alone with a fresh deployment.

items.question: Do you offer ongoing maintenance?
items.answer: Yes. I offer maintenance and development plans covering updates, bug fixes, performance improvements, and new features as your product evolves.

items.question: Can you train my team to manage the site?
items.answer: Yes. I provide documentation and walkthroughs so your team understands the system and can manage content or basic operations confidently.

items.question: What if I need new features later?
items.answer: New features can be added as separate projects or as part of a maintenance agreement. Clean code means extending functionality is usually straightforward and predictable.

items.question: What happens if my website goes down after launch?
items.answer: First, my builds have monitoring, so I usually know before you do. Second, the most common cause of a "dead" site is mundane: an expired domain or hosting payment, worth checking your inbox first. Statically rendered sites without plugins have very few ways to fall over, which is a real advantage of building without them. If something does break in what I built, fixing it is on me, not billed to you.

items.question: How often should my website be updated after launch?
items.answer: Separate two things. Content: whenever your offer changes, and Google likes signs of life. Technical updates: a custom-coded site needs almost none, because there are no plugins demanding weekly patches, which is a structural advantage over WordPress. A sensible rhythm is a content review every few months and a design refresh every couple of years as the brand evolves.

items.question: What is included in a monthly maintenance plan?
items.answer: Here is the honest answer: for the sites I build, you mostly do not need one, and I do not sell subscriptions. There are no plugins to update, hosting and domain cost a fixed small amount yearly, and monitoring is included. When you want changes or new features, you pay for that specific work. Compare that with maintenance retainers that mostly bill you for updating the plugins the builder chose to depend on.

items.question: Will my site need a redesign eventually, and how soon?
items.answer: Design ages: most business sites feel dated after three to five years, sometimes sooner in fast-moving industries. What should not age is the foundation: with clean code, a redesign is a reskin of working machinery instead of a rebuild from zero. That is a deliberate reason to avoid platform lock-in now, because it halves the cost of every future refresh.

items.question: Do you keep backups of my site in case something goes wrong?
items.answer: Yes, structurally: the code lives in version control with full history, so any previous state can be restored in minutes and deployments can be rolled back with one command. Managed content has its own service-side backups. This beats the classic backup-plugin approach, where the safety net is itself a plugin that can fail.

items.question: What happens if my site gets hacked after launch?
items.answer: The realistic answer for my builds: the attack surface is tiny, because the classic entry point (outdated plugins and themes) does not exist, and security headers plus HTTPS are configured from day one. If something did happen, the site can be redeployed clean from version control in minutes, which is the fastest recovery path there is. Prevention through architecture beats cleanup services every time.

seo.h1: FlowMate: AI Email SaaS Built Solo in 6 Months
seo.ogTitle: FlowMate - AI Email SaaS Case Study | Max Mendes
seo.ogDescription: Solo-built SaaS unifying 4 communication platforms with AI assistance. Technical deep-dive into architecture, security, and lessons learned.

schema.description: Technical case study of FlowMate, an AI-powered email management SaaS platform built with Next.js, React, and multiple AI providers.
schema.author: Max Mendes

hero.title: FlowMate: From Email Chaos to AI-Powered Clarity
hero.subtitle: How I built a production SaaS that turns 200 emails into 5 decisions
hero.notice: Personal learning project. The product is paused; the landing page remains live as a demo.

sections.title: The Problem
content: Working in IT support, I was drowning in email. Multiple inboxes demanding attention. Constant context-switching between Gmail, Slack, and Telegram. Customer service emails piling up faster than I could respond. Hours lost every day just managing communication instead of actually doing meaningful work.

The pain was real: jumping between 4+ platforms daily, writing the same types of responses repeatedly, missing important emails buried under newsletters, and no way to broadcast updates across all channels without copying and pasting the same message four times.

I didn't need another email client with a prettier interface. I needed something that would actually reduce the work. A unified hub where AI handles the boring parts so I could focus on decisions, not inbox management.

sections.title: The Solution
content: FlowMate unifies Gmail, Outlook, Slack, and Telegram into a single intelligent hub. AI handles categorization, generates professional replies, summarizes long threads, and lets you broadcast to all platforms with one click.

The core idea: reduce 200 emails to 5 actual decisions. Let AI categorize what's urgent, what needs action, what's just FYI. Draft responses you can send or edit. Broadcast announcements everywhere without writing four different messages.

highlight.quote: Best feature is the broadcast, how easy it is to send to all platforms with different styles for each one automatically.
highlight.author: Early User Feedback

sections.title: Technical Challenges

subsections.title: Security Was Non-Negotiable
content: Handling people's email means handling their most sensitive data. OAuth tokens, personal messages, business communications. One security breach and the product is dead.

Every decision prioritized protection: AES-256-GCM encryption for all OAuth tokens with PBKDF2 key derivation. Row Level Security policies on every user table in the database. CSRF protection using 256-bit random state parameters. Rate limiting at 200 requests per minute per IP. Middleware-level bot blocking for 50+ known malicious crawlers. Constant-time comparisons to prevent timing attacks on authentication flows.

subsections.title: Multi-Provider OAuth Complexity
content: Each platform has different OAuth flows, different scopes, different edge cases. Gmail requires validating that the authenticated account matches the user's email. Slack needs signed state parameters to work in incognito mode. Telegram uses phone-based authentication with 2FA support instead of standard OAuth.
I built a unified token management system that handles all four providers with encrypted storage, automatic token refresh, and graceful degradation when individual services fail.

subsections.title: State Management at Scale
content: FlowMate has 10 different views, 4 platforms syncing simultaneously, real-time updates, draft management, AI chat history, and user preferences, all needing to stay in sync without performance degradation.

The solution combined several patterns: optimistic sync (save locally first, sync to database in background), IndexedDB paired with Supabase for offline-first architecture with cloud as source of truth, per-user storage keys for multi-tenant isolation, and circuit breakers with exponential backoff for graceful API failure handling.

subsections.title: AI Cost Optimization
content: AI features are expensive at scale. Running every email through a large language model would bankrupt the project before it launched. I implemented a model fallback chain: Claude 3 Haiku first at $0.25 per million tokens, escalating to Claude 3.5 Haiku at $0.80 only when needed, with heuristic fallbacks for simple categorization tasks.
The result: 99.5% reliability on natural language email queries while keeping costs sustainable for a subscription business.

link.text: Learn more about AI integration services →

sections.title: The Build
content: Solo development from architecture through deployment. Every decision (database schema, authentication flow, AI prompts, payment integration, error handling) made by one person with full context of the entire system.

This approach has tradeoffs. Slower than a team, but no communication overhead. Every part of the codebase fits together because one mind designed it. Technical debt is minimal because I had to live with every shortcut.

stackDecisions.why: Server-side rendering, API routes in one codebase, App Router for modern patterns
Row Level Security policies, real-time subscriptions, generous free tier for MVP
Better social login experience than Supabase Auth, faster integration
Switch between Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek without code changes
Industry standard, reliable webhooks, good documentation
Zero-config deploys, edge functions, built-in analytics

sections.title: What Got Built
features: Unified inbox for Gmail, Outlook, Slack, Telegram
AI categorization: Priority, Action Required, Newsletters, Social, FYI
Smart replies with professional tone matching
Multi-turn AI chat with conversation memory
Broadcast system: compose once, send everywhere

sections.title: Results & Lessons
content: User feedback validated the core thesis: Broadcast was the killer feature. The ability to compose once and send to Email, Slack, and Telegram, with AI generating platform-specific variations automatically, saved the most time. I invested heavily in making that flow feel effortless.

The strongest signal: users understand the entire app in about 30 minutes. No commands to memorize. No complex onboarding flow. Connect your accounts and start working. Simplicity was harder to build than complexity, but worth it.

lessonsLearned.title: Ship Smaller, Validate Faster
lessonsLearned.content: Launched with 10 views and 4 integrations. Could have validated the core idea with Gmail + AI alone, then expanded based on real usage patterns.

lessonsLearned.title: Users Find What You Miss
lessonsLearned.content: Built in isolation for months. Real users found bugs and UX friction points within days that I'd completely missed. Earlier feedback would have saved rework.

lessonsLearned.title: Features Aren't Value
lessonsLearned.content: Spent time building features before nailing the messaging. The broadcast feature was valuable because it solved a clear problem, not because it was technically impressive.
